nameMeanings.name: Mattern
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German (mainly Silesia) and Dutch (of German origin): from the medieval personal name Matern, Latin Maternus (derived from Latin mater ‘mother’), borne by a legendary Christian saint. This surname is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine).

variants.name: Mathern
meanings: German: variant of   Mattern  . This surname is also found in France (mainly Alsace).

variants.name: Ternes
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Aloys, Balzer, Hans, Kurt, Mathias, Otto.
German: from a short form of the personal name Maternus, from Latin, ‘the maternal (i.e. motherly) one’. Compare Mattern .

variants.name: Matern
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erwin, Ingo, Kurt, Otto, Wilhelmina.
German and Dutch (of German origin): variant of   Mattern  . This surname is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine).

variants.name: Materna
meanings: Polish and Czech: apparently a nickname from Latin materna, feminine form of the adjective maternus ‘maternal’. It may also be from this Latin word used as a personal name (see Mattern ).
Czech: probably, in some cases at least, an altered form under the influence of the Latin word materna (see 1 above) of the personal name Matěj; see Matthew .

nameMeanings.name: CARL
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From an old-fashioned German spelling variant of Karl , the German version of Charles . It is now increasingly used in the English-speaking world, and for some reason is particularly popular in Wales.

nameMeanings.name: Boris
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: from the Tartar nickname Bogoris ‘small’. Later, however, it was taken to be a shortened form of the Russian name Borislav, from bor ‘battle’ + slav ‘glory’. The name was borne in the 9th century by a ruler of Bulgaria who converted his kingdom to Christianity and gave shelter to disciples of saints Cyril and Methodius when they were expelled from Moravia. The name was also borne by a 10th-century Russian saint, son of Prince Vladimir of Kiev and brother of St Gleb . It is as a result of his influence that Boris is one of the very few non-classical names that the Orthodox Church allows to be taken as a baptismal name (although the saint himself bore the baptismal name Romanus). It is borne by the British journalist and politician Boris Johnson ( b. 1964 ).
